---
title: "Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction: A Guide for Statisticians"
author: "Tyler Hansen"
date: "2-26-2026"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    numbered sections: false
bibliography: references.bib
suppress-bibliography: true
---

# Introduction

High-dimensional data (data with many columns) is common in fields like biology, medicine, and engineering. When data has too many columns, models become slow, visualization becomes impossible, and noise drowns out meaningful patterns. An autoencoder is a neural network that compresses your data automatically, keeping the important structure and discarding the rest. This makes autoencoders useful for visualization, noise reduction, and finding hidden patterns that simpler methods like PCA miss.

This guide walks you through building and using an autoencoder from start to finish using a real dataset you can run yourself.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Prerequisites

## Knowledge

-   **PCA** — autoencoders are a nonlinear extension of PCA, familiarity with PCA is essential to continue
-   **Basic Python** — reading and running scripts is sufficent, no deep learning experience needed

## Software

Choose one deep learning library, you do not need both:

-   **PyTorch** — more flexible, popular in research
-   **Keras** — simpler syntax, faster to get started

You will also need **Python 3.10** and **conda** (Miniconda from [docs.conda.io](https://docs.conda.io/en/latest/miniconda.html)).

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# What Is an Autoencoder?

An autoencoder is a neural network trained to compress data and then reconstruct it. The network has three parts:

-   **Encoder** — compresses your data into fewer numbers
-   **Bottleneck** — the compressed version (this is what you keep)
-   **Decoder** — reconstructs the original data during training only

```{python}
#| label: fig-arch
#| fig-cap: "The three parts of an autoencoder. Data is compressed at the bottleneck and then reconstructed. After training, only the encoder and bottleneck output are used."
#| echo: false
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 2))
for x, label, color in [(1,"Input","gray"),(3,"Encoder","steelblue"),(5,"Bottleneck","red"),(7,"Decoder","green"),(9,"Output","gray")]:
    ax.scatter(x, 0, s=1500, color=color, zorder=3)
    ax.text(x, -0.3, label, ha="center", fontsize=9)
for x1, x2 in [(1.2,2.8),(3.2,4.8),(5.2,6.8),(7.2,8.8)]:
    ax.annotate("", xy=(x2,0), xytext=(x1,0), arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->"))
ax.axis("off"); ax.set_ylim(-0.6,0.4); plt.show()
```

The network trains by trying to reconstruct its own input. Because everything must pass through the narrow bottleneck, it is forced to learn a compact summary of the data. Under specific conditions an autoencoder is mathematically equivalent to PCA [@baldi1989] — making it a natural nonlinear extension of a tool you already know.

## Should You Use an Autoencoder?

**Use PCA if:** your dataset is small, you need interpretable components, or you want something fast with no training required.

**Use an autoencoder if:** PCA reconstructions look poor, your data has complex structure (images, sensor readings, expressions), or you have at least a few thousand rows [@hinton2006].

::: callout-tip
When in doubt, try PCA first. If it works well enough, use it. Autoencoders require more setup and tuning.
:::

|                          | PCA    | Autoencoder |
|--------------------------|--------|-------------|
| Finds nonlinear patterns | No     | Yes         |
| Output usable in a model | Yes    | Yes         |
| Needs training           | No     | Yes         |
| Components have meaning  | Yes    | No          |
| Works on images / audio  | Poorly | Yes         |

: Comparison of PCA and autoencoders {#tbl-comparison}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# The Dataset

This guide uses the **penguins** dataset, available in the `palmerpenguins` Python package. It contains physical measurements of 344 penguins across three species. It is small enough to run quickly on any machine and structured enough to show meaningful compression.

``` python
from palmerpenguins import load_penguins

df = load_penguins().dropna()

X = df[["bill_length_mm", "bill_depth_mm", "flipper_length_mm", "body_mass_g"]].values
species = df["species"].values   # for coloring plots later

print(f"Data shape: {X.shape}")   # (333, 4)
```

Install the package with:

``` bash
pip install palmerpenguins
```

The four columns are bill length, bill depth, flipper length, and body mass, these are our input features. We will compress these four dimensions down to two, then compare the result to PCA.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Setup

## Create an Environment

``` bash
conda create -n ae-env python=3.10
conda activate ae-env
```

::: callout-important
Every time you open a new terminal, rerun the activate command before working.
:::

## Install Packages

**PyTorch (CPU — works on any machine):**

``` bash
pip install torch==2.2.0 --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cpu
pip install numpy==1.26.4 pandas==2.2.1 scikit-learn==1.4.1 matplotlib==3.8.3 palmerpenguins
```

**Keras / TensorFlow (Windows or Linux):**

``` bash
pip install tensorflow==2.15.0
pip install numpy==1.26.4 pandas==2.2.1 scikit-learn==1.4.1 matplotlib==3.8.3 palmerpenguins
```

::: callout-warning
**Mac with M1/M2/M3?** Use `tensorflow-macos==2.15.0` and `tensorflow-metal` instead of `tensorflow`.
:::


## Set Random Seeds {#sec-seeds}

Put this at the top of every script so results are reproducible:

**PyTorch:**

``` python
import random, numpy as np, torch
random.seed(42); np.random.seed(42); torch.manual_seed(42)
torch.backends.cudnn.deterministic = True
torch.backends.cudnn.benchmark = False
```

**Keras:**

``` python
import os, random, numpy as np, tensorflow as tf
os.environ['PYTHONHASHSEED'] = '42'
random.seed(42); np.random.seed(42); tf.random.set_seed(42)
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Prepare Your Data

-   **All numeric** — our penguin columns are already numeric
-   **No missing values** — removed above with `.dropna()`
-   **Scaled to \[0, 1\]** — neural networks are sensitive to scale

::: callout-important
Always split **before** scaling. Scaling first leaks validation information into training.
:::

``` python
from sklearn.preprocessing import MinMaxScaler
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

X_train, X_val, species_train, species_val = train_test_split(
    X, species, test_size=0.2, random_state=42
)

scaler  = MinMaxScaler()
X_train = scaler.fit_transform(X_train)
X_val   = scaler.transform(X_val)
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Build and Train

## PyTorch

``` python
import torch, torch.nn as nn
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader, TensorDataset

class Autoencoder(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, input_dim, k):
        super().__init__()
        self.encoder = nn.Sequential(nn.Linear(input_dim, 32), nn.ReLU(), nn.Linear(32, k))
        self.decoder = nn.Sequential(nn.Linear(k, 32), nn.ReLU(), nn.Linear(32, input_dim), nn.Sigmoid())
    def forward(self, x): return self.decoder(self.encoder(x))
    def encode(self, x):  return self.encoder(x)

X_tr  = torch.tensor(X_train, dtype=torch.float32)
X_vl  = torch.tensor(X_val,   dtype=torch.float32)
model = Autoencoder(input_dim=4, k=2)
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
loss_fn   = nn.MSELoss()
train_losses, val_losses = [], []

for epoch in range(200):
    model.train()
    for (batch,) in DataLoader(TensorDataset(X_tr), batch_size=32, shuffle=True):
        optimizer.zero_grad(); loss = loss_fn(model(batch), batch)
        loss.backward(); optimizer.step()
    model.eval()
    with torch.no_grad():
        train_losses.append(loss_fn(model(X_tr), X_tr).item())
        val_losses.append(loss_fn(model(X_vl), X_vl).item())
```

## Keras

``` python
from tensorflow import keras
from tensorflow.keras import layers

def build(input_dim, k):
    inp = keras.Input(shape=(input_dim,))
    x   = layers.Dense(32, activation="relu")(inp)
    z   = layers.Dense(k, name="bottleneck")(x)
    x   = layers.Dense(32, activation="relu")(z)
    out = layers.Dense(input_dim, activation="sigmoid")(x)
    return keras.Model(inp, out), keras.Model(inp, z)

autoencoder, encoder = build(input_dim=4, k=2)
autoencoder.compile(optimizer="adam", loss="mse")
autoencoder.fit(X_train, X_train, epochs=200, batch_size=32,
                validation_data=(X_val, X_val))
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Check Your Training Curves

Always plot losses after training — this is your main diagnostic.

``` python
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
plt.plot(train_losses, label="Train")
plt.plot(val_losses,   label="Validation")
plt.xlabel("Epoch"); plt.ylabel("Loss"); plt.legend(); plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-curves
#| fig-cap: "Good training (left), overfitting (middle), not learning (right). You want the left pattern."
#| echo: false
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

e = np.arange(60)
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(10, 3))
axes[0].plot(0.4*np.exp(-e/15)+0.03, label="Train"); axes[0].plot(0.4*np.exp(-e/15)+0.06, label="Val"); axes[0].set_title("Good")
axes[1].plot(0.4*np.exp(-e/10)+0.01, label="Train"); axes[1].plot(0.1+e*0.003, label="Val"); axes[1].set_title("Overfitting")
axes[2].plot(np.repeat(0.28,60), label="Train"); axes[2].plot(np.repeat(0.29,60), label="Val"); axes[2].set_title("Not Learning")
for ax in axes: ax.legend(fontsize=8); ax.set_xlabel("Epoch"); ax.set_ylabel("Loss")
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Extract and Compare to PCA

After training, extract the bottleneck output and compare it to PCA on the same data.

**PyTorch:**

``` python
model.eval()
with torch.no_grad():
    Z_ae = model.encode(X_tr).numpy()
```

**Keras:**

``` python
Z_ae = encoder.predict(X_train)
```

Now run PCA for comparison:

``` python
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
Z_pca = PCA(n_components=2).fit_transform(X_train)
```

Plot both side by side, colored by species:

``` python
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

colors = {"Adelie": "#E41A1C", "Chinstrap": "#377EB8", "Gentoo": "#4DAF4A"}
c = [colors[s] for s in species_train]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 4))
axes[0].scatter(Z_pca[:,0], Z_pca[:,1], c=c, alpha=0.7, s=30)
axes[0].set_title("PCA"); axes[0].set_xlabel("Component 1"); axes[0].set_ylabel("Component 2")
axes[1].scatter(Z_ae[:,0],  Z_ae[:,1],  c=c, alpha=0.7, s=30)
axes[1].set_title("Autoencoder"); axes[1].set_xlabel("Dimension 1"); axes[1].set_ylabel("Dimension 2")
for sp, col in colors.items():
    axes[1].scatter([], [], color=col, label=sp)
axes[1].legend()
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-latent
#| fig-cap: "Illustrative comparison of PCA (left) vs autoencoder (right) on the penguins dataset, colored by species. Both methods separate the three species, but the autoencoder may find a different arrangement since it learns nonlinear structure."
#| echo: false
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

rng = np.random.default_rng(5)
colors = ["#E41A1C", "#377EB8", "#4DAF4A"]
labels = ["Adelie", "Chinstrap", "Gentoo"]
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 4))

for i, (cx, cy) in enumerate([(-1.5,0.5),(0.5,1.0),(1.5,-0.5)]):
    n = [150, 68, 119][i]
    axes[0].scatter(rng.normal(cx,0.5,n), rng.normal(cy,0.4,n), color=colors[i], alpha=0.7, s=20, label=labels[i])
axes[0].set_title("PCA"); axes[0].set_xlabel("Component 1"); axes[0].set_ylabel("Component 2"); axes[0].legend(fontsize=8)

for i, (cx, cy) in enumerate([(-2,0),(0,1.8),(2,0)]):
    n = [150, 68, 119][i]
    axes[1].scatter(rng.normal(cx,0.38,n), rng.normal(cy,0.38,n), color=colors[i], alpha=0.7, s=20, label=labels[i])
axes[1].set_title("Autoencoder"); axes[1].set_xlabel("Dimension 1"); axes[1].set_ylabel("Dimension 2"); axes[1].legend(fontsize=8)

plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

::: callout-note
Unlike PCA components, bottleneck dimensions have no natural ordering or interpretation. Judge the result by whether it helps your clustering, classification, or visualization.
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Choosing the Bottleneck Size

There is no scree plot for autoencoders. Train models with several bottleneck sizes and look for the elbow where adding more dimensions stops helping much.

``` python
results = {}
for k in [2, 4, 8, 16]:
    model = Autoencoder(input_dim=4, k=k)
    optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
    for epoch in range(200):
        for (batch,) in DataLoader(TensorDataset(X_tr), batch_size=32, shuffle=True):
            optimizer.zero_grad(); loss = loss_fn(model(batch), batch)
            loss.backward(); optimizer.step()
    model.eval()
    with torch.no_grad():
        results[k] = loss_fn(model(X_vl), X_vl).item()

plt.plot(list(results.keys()), list(results.values()), marker="o")
plt.xlabel("Bottleneck size (k)"); plt.ylabel("Validation loss")
plt.title("Choose k at the elbow"); plt.show()
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Troubleshooting

| Problem | Fix |
|------------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| Loss does not decrease | Check scaling- try `lr=1e-4` |
| Validation loss much higher than training | Use early stopping- shrink the model |
| Poor reconstructions | Increase bottleneck size |
| Different results every run | Set random seeds — see @sec-seeds |
| Import errors | Re-run `conda activate ae-env` |

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Additional Resources

All of the following are cited in @nalisnick2023 and are appropriate for readers with a statistics background.

-   @goodfellow2016 — the standard deep learning textbook; Chapters 1 and 14 cover autoencoders in depth
-   @doersch2021 — a readable tutorial on variational autoencoders, a probabilistic extension of the standard autoencoder
-   @nalisnick2023 — the source article for this guide; provides broader statistical context for all of deep learning

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Summary

An autoencoder compresses your data by learning to reconstruct its own input through a narrow bottleneck. The bottleneck output is your compressed feature matrix, use it like PCA scores for clustering, prediction, or visualization.

The steps are:

1.  Confirm your data is numeric, complete, and scaled to \[0, 1\]
2.  Set up a pinned environment and fix all random seeds
3.  Build an encoder–bottleneck–decoder network
4.  Train and check the loss curves
5.  Extract the encoder output and compare to PCA
6.  Search for the right bottleneck size using validation loss

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# References {.unnumbered}

Full citations are on the [References](references.qmd) page.
